‚ÄčPersonalized medicine‚Äč
‚Äč‚óŹ‚Äč ‚ÄčGeneral idea and benefits: a 10% increase in knowing the right medicine to try can help millions of‚Äč
‚Äčpeople get faster and better treatment for a disease‚Äč
‚Äč‚óŹ‚Äč ‚ÄčSpecific logic with NE receptor vs. DA receptor genetic deficiencies and how that can inform ADHD‚Äč
‚Äčmedication choices (targeted towards deficiency)‚Äč
‚Äč‚óŹ‚Äč ‚ÄčLimitations: even with all genetic knowledge, no guarantee of the predicted medicine working. For‚Äč
‚Äčexample, many people who have no dopamine related genetic risk factors for ADHD still respond well‚Äč
‚Äčto Ritalin and other DAT blockers to treat their ADHD‚Äč
‚ÄčDopamine deficit ‚Üí increase dopamine ‚Üí DAT blocker‚Äč
‚ÄčNorepinephrine deficit ‚Üí increase norepinephrine ‚Üí NET blocker‚Äč
‚ÄčHeritability‚Äč
‚Äč-‚Äč
‚ÄčHeritability (genetic contribution) = (rateID - rateFRAT)/(relID - relFRAT) = (rateID - rateFRAT)/0.5 =‚Äč
‚Äč2*(rateID - rateFRAT)‚Äč
‚Äč-‚Äč
‚ÄčRelatedness:‚Äč
‚Äč-‚Äč
‚ÄčID twins are 100%‚Äč
‚Äč-‚Äč
‚ÄčFraternal twins, parents/children, full siblings are all 50%‚Äč
‚Äč-‚Äč
‚ÄčHalf siblings, aunt/uncle/niece/nephew, grandparent/grandchild are all 25%‚Äč
‚Äč-‚Äč
‚ÄčCousins are 12.5%‚Äč
‚Äč-‚Äč
‚ÄčConcordance: 2 people same disorder‚Äč
‚Äč-‚Äč
‚ÄčCalculating observed concordance rates:‚Äč
‚Äč-‚Äč
‚ÄčExample 1: out of a group of 400 identical twins where one has a disorder, if we find that the‚Äč
‚Äčother has the disorder in 300 of the cases, then the concordance rate = 300 / 400=  75%‚Äč
‚ÄčTourette Syndrome‚Äč
‚ÄčBasics: Motor and vocal tics present for at least a year, symptoms presenting under the age of 18.‚Äč
‚ÄčDopamine‚Äč
‚ÄčDopamine in Striatum‚Äč
‚ÄčIt inhibits stop cells and excites go cells, resulting in‚Äč‚Äčincreased movement‚Äč
‚ÄčDopamine in VTA -> Nucleus accumbens‚Äč
‚ÄčCauses pleasure, brain reward system, reinforces behaviours we want to repeat‚Äč
‚ÄčDopamine in VTA -> Frontal Lobe‚Äč
‚ÄčPromotes attention, thinking, self control, low dopamine -> ADHD symptoms‚Äč
‚ÄčOCD‚Äč
‚ÄčObsessions are intrusive thoughts that are anxiety-inducing (unpleasant, worrisome) AND persists or‚Äč
‚Äčrecoccurs. Compulsions are an urge to do a particular behavior in response to an obsession. Some examples‚Äč
‚Äčmight include checking, washing, counting, tapping, gestures, needing to ask/confess or hoarding.‚Äč
‚Äč‚ÄúOCD cycle‚ÄĚ: Intrusive thoughts,‚Äč‚Äčobsessions, anxiety, compulsions, temporary relief‚Äč, re-emergence of‚Äč
‚Äčintrusive/worrisome thoughts.‚Äč
‚ÄčIn addition to talk therapy, the most common first medication for OCD is a selective-serotonin reuptake inhibitor‚Äč
‚Äč(SSRI), which blocks the serotonin transporter (SerT, the protein the removes serotonin from synapses and‚Äč
‚Äčbrings it back into the presynaptic terminal); this has the effect of extending the serotonin signal and can help‚Äč
‚Äčwith many types of anxiety and depression including the anxiety in OCD.‚Äč

title_guess: ‚ÄčFirst question for Welch & colleagues study: how does removal of the Sapap3 gene (knockout mice) affect‚Äč
extracted_text:
‚ÄčFirst question for Welch & colleagues study: how does removal of the Sapap3 gene (knockout mice) affect‚Äč
‚ÄčOCD-like behavior in mice? Result: mice show‚Äč‚ÄčOCD-like behaviors‚Äč‚Äč(excessive grooming, anxiety)‚Äč
‚ÄčBasal ganglia activity is different in patients with OCD vs. controls (in complicated ways that you don‚Äôt need to‚Äč
‚Äčknow). That, connected with the changes in OFC activity in OCD (above), leads to the second question in the‚Äč
‚ÄčWelch study.‚Äč
‚ÄčBasal ganglia‚Äč‚Äčand‚Äč‚ÄčOFC (orbitofrontal cortex)‚Äč‚Äčshow abnormal activity in OCD.‚Äč
‚ÄčThese regions help control‚Äč‚Äčhabits and behavior.‚Äč
‚ÄčKnow about the second question of the Welch study: How do mice with OCD-like behaviors differ from control‚Äč
‚Äčmice in OFC to Striatum connection? Know the methods for studying that question. As with all questions, you‚Äč
‚Äčshould also be prepared to apply similar methods to a new situation.‚Äč
‚ÄčBrain recordings‚Äč‚Äčto measure synapse strength‚Äč
‚ÄčCompare‚Äč‚ÄčOFC‚Äďstriatum signaling‚Äč‚Äčin mutant vs. normal‚Äč‚Äčmice‚Äč
‚ÄčFound‚Äč‚Äčweaker synaptic connections‚Äč‚Äčin OCD-like mice‚Äč
‚ÄčGroups: Same 2 groups, control mice (normal) and knockout mice (missing Sapap3 Gene)‚Äč
‚ÄčManipulation:  Stimulate axons from cortex.‚Äč
‚ÄčMeasured variable: Record voltage in Striatum when OFC stimulated‚Äč
‚ÄčPossible results: If stronger, then bigger response. If weaker, then smaller response. If no change, then same‚Äč
‚Äčvoltage response.‚Äč
‚ÄčResults: The response in KO striatum neurons is weaker.‚Äč
‚ÄčInterpretation: Weaker synapse from OFC -> Striatum in OCD. This would relate to the idea that people with‚Äč
‚ÄčOCD have difficulty breaking out of their obsessive-compulsive cycles, perhaps due to the lack of synapse‚Äč
‚Äčstrength.‚Äč
‚ÄčADHD continued (and some highly relevant material from unit 1 to remember‚Äč
‚ÄčRoles of dopamine‚Äč
‚ÄčVTA -> Frontal lobes (relationship to sustained attention and also to seeking more stimulation causes‚Äč
‚Äčinattentive/attention switching)‚Äč
‚ÄčRole of NE: similar to dopamine, receptors in frontal lobe important for symptoms‚Äč
‚ÄčADHD genetics‚Äč
‚ÄčUnderactive Dopamine receptors - Use DAT blocker (Ritalin), less dopamine produced and released‚Äč
‚ÄčUnderactive NE receptors - Use NET blocker (Atomoxetine)‚Äč
‚ÄčADHD treatments‚Äč
‚ÄčRitalin: dopamine transporter (DAT) blocker‚Äč
‚ÄčComparison to cocaine: difference in which areas are affected‚Äč
‚ÄčOverdose of Ritalin:‚Äč
‚ÄčModerate overdose -> Too much dopamine in reward and movement circuits (SnC-Striatum and‚Äč
‚ÄčVTA-nAC circuits), causing increased hyperactivity and pleasure‚Äč
‚ÄčSevere overdose -> excessive neuron firing (too much dopamine), leading to seizures, respiratory‚Äč
‚Äčand/or cardiovascular failure and death‚Äč
